title: load_balancer_failure
description: A load balancer failure causes all web traffic to concentrate on a single backend, leading to overload.

timeline.title: Load Balancer Alert
timeline.description: Monitoring shows one of two load balancer nodes has become unresponsive. Traffic is now routing through a single node.

timeline.title: Backend Overload Begins
timeline.description: The single active load balancer is sending all traffic to all backends unevenly. One backend is receiving 90% of traffic.

timeline.title: Service Degradation
timeline.description: The overloaded backend begins returning 503 errors. User-facing error rate climbs to 25%.

timeline.title: Load Balancer Diagnosis
timeline.description: Engineers investigate the failed load balancer node and identify a configuration corruption after an automated update.

timeline.title: Load Balancer Node Restored
timeline.description: The failed node is restored with a clean configuration. Active-active load balancing resumes.

timeline.title: Traffic Balanced and Services Restored
timeline.description: Traffic is now distributed correctly across all backends. Error rates return to zero.

node-1.title: Load Balancer Node Failure
node-1.situation: One of your two active-active load balancer nodes has failed. All traffic is routing through the surviving node, which is distributing traffic unevenly — one backend is receiving 90% of requests and is returning 503 errors. Users are experiencing a 25% error rate.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Adjust the surviving load balancer configuration to distribute traffic more evenly across healthy backends, then diagnose the failed node while restoring user experience.

options.label: B
options.text: Take the overloaded backend offline to reduce error rates, further concentrating traffic on fewer servers.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for the failed load balancer node to auto-recover without taking any immediate action.

node-2.title: Failed Node Diagnosis
node-2.situation: Traffic is now balanced across backends and error rates have dropped to under 2%. You are examining the failed load balancer node. Its management interface is accessible but the load balancing process is in a crash loop. Configuration files show corruption consistent with a partial automated update.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Restore the failed node configuration from the known-good backup taken before the automated update, verify the configuration is clean, and prepare to reintroduce the node to the active-active cluster.

options.label: B
options.text: Delete and recreate the failed node from scratch using only default configuration settings without the production-specific configuration.

node-3.title: Node Reintroduction
node-3.situation: The failed node has been reconfigured from backup. It is ready to be reintroduced to the active-active cluster. You need to do this without causing another traffic disruption.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Add the restored node back to the cluster with a low traffic weight (10%), monitor that it handles traffic correctly for 5 minutes, then gradually increase its weight to 50% for full active-active operation.

options.label: B
options.text: Add the restored node back immediately at full 50% traffic weight without a warm-up period.

node-4.title: Prevention and Monitoring
node-4.situation: Both load balancer nodes are active and traffic is balanced correctly. You need to investigate why the automated update caused configuration corruption and prevent it from happening again.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Review the automated update process, implement pre-update configuration backups and post-update health checks, add a canary step that updates one node and validates before updating the second, and document the incident.

options.label: B
options.text: Disable automated updates entirely and rely only on manual updates performed during maintenance windows.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You quickly mitigated user impact by rebalancing traffic on the surviving node, restored the failed node using a clean configuration backup, reintroduced it with a gradual warm-up, and improved the automated update process to prevent recurrence.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
